title: Budget vs. Actual: Tracking Construction Finances and Draw Management
summary: Erin walked participants through the Budget vs. Actual spreadsheet used in her construction business, covering cost codes, modifications tracking, draw schedules, and the percent complete column. The session also addressed pricing certainty communication with clients, billing structures for project managers and superintendents, and strategies for connecting with architects and designers. Participants shared how they had applied the pricing certainty framework in their own client meetings with positive results.

teachings.explanation: The budget vs. actual tracker has four key components: the original budget, a modifications column for any changes during construction, a total budget column (budget + modifications), and draw columns tracking previous draws, current draw, total draws, balance to finish, and percent complete. This gives owners and clients a real-time snapshot of project finances.
teachings.quote: Budget plus modifications equals the total budget. That's where we are.

teachings.explanation: All changes during construction must be captured in a dedicated modifications column. Erin emphasizes assigning one person to own the modifications process to avoid confusion, loss of data, or complexity that comes from bouncing responsibility between team members.
teachings.quote: I want one person to manage this. I don't want it to bounce between people too much because then we lose it.

teachings.concept: Percent Complete as a Financial Alignment Tool
teachings.explanation: The percent complete column tracks what percentage of the total budget has been drawn. It exists to ensure the money being requested from a client is proportional to the actual construction progress on site. Misalignment between construction progress and draw requests is a major source of project financial problems.
teachings.quote: Where projects get in trouble in big trouble is when the percent done on the construction site is 20 but the money we're asking from a client is at 70 percent. That's off.

teachings.concept: Price Certainty as Emotional Certainty for Clients
teachings.explanation: Assigning a certainty percentage to each line item in a conceptual budget is not about giving a range of possible costs but about creating transparency and managing client expectations. The goal is to honestly communicate what is known versus unknown, reducing client overconfidence and building trust.
teachings.quote: It's more about creating emotional certainty really. So if you just keep that in mind.

teachings.explanation: Before any draw or invoice is issued to a client, multiple checkpoints must be passed: project managers approve the work, money managers compile the invoice, the homeowner is satisfied with the work, and the architect and designer sign off. This multi-step approval process prevents disputes and maintains trust.
teachings.quote: We don't issue a draw or an invoice to a client until it's done and it's approved. A thumbs up from everybody.

teachings.concept: Projected Budget as a Separate Working Copy
teachings.explanation: If a team wants to model what a budget might look like with unapproved modifications or hypothetical changes, that should be done on a separate copy of the spreadsheet. The official budget vs. actual document should only contain real, approved numbers.
teachings.quote: Unless it's a real thing and it's really approved, we don't load it into this spreadsheet. These are actual facts.

teachings.concept: Time, Money, and People as the Three Project Drivers
teachings.explanation: When evaluating billing models, staffing decisions, or project structures, Erin uses the framework of time, money, and people as the three core variables that drive every project. Any billing or fee model must account for all three, including the skill level and experience of the people involved.
teachings.quote: When we look at our projects it's time money and people. Those are the drivers. Time money people, that's in the equation every time.

teachings.concept: Repeat Relationships with Architects and Designers as a Long-Term Strategy
teachings.explanation: As a builder matures, the most efficient source of high-quality work shifts toward repeat collaborations with architects and designers rather than constantly sourcing new clients. This takes years to develop but produces highly aligned, efficient project teams.
teachings.quote: Most of our business, 90 percent, is repeat with architects and designers just because now we're gelled as teams.

actionable_advice: Assign one person on your team to own all modifications tracking and give them clear authority over that column to prevent data loss and confusion.
Use the notes column in the budget vs. actual spreadsheet extensively so every team member and the owner can see the current status and reasoning behind each line item.
Do not include projected or unapproved modifications in the official budget vs. actual spreadsheet; use a separate copy for hypothetical scenarios.
Before submitting any draw to a client, ensure all checkpoints are met: project manager approval, money manager review, owner review, homeowner satisfaction, and architect and designer sign-off.
When applying pricing certainty percentages, prepare specific examples of undeveloped details to walk clients through, so they understand why a line item is rated at 50 percent rather than higher.

Define clearly in your contract what the start and end points of a project are when using a fixed monthly fee model, especially since the final weeks of a project still require significant work even after a homeowner moves in.

If you want to scale up your spreadsheet complexity, hire a credentialed CPA or someone with big four accounting experience rather than building complex linked workbooks without financial controls, and include balance-check columns throughout.

To connect with new architects and designers, leverage your high-touch trade partners such as wood flooring specialists and home automation professionals who work across many projects and can introduce you by name.

Apply the pricing certainty framework to timeline and lead times as well as cost, so clients understand that schedule delays are tied to their own decision-making pace rather than the builder being a bottleneck.

When considering a fixed fee billing model for project managers and superintendents, analyze a full year of project cash flow to determine what a healthy monthly number looks like before committing to a rate.
client_challenges: A participant struggled with how to determine what pricing certainty percentage to assign to various line items and whether the percentage implies a cost range to the client.

A participant managing a 17-home subdivision was trying to find a fair and consistent billing structure for project managers and superintendents that would work across both spec homes and client homes without clients feeling they were being charged during slow periods.
A participant worried that clients might be frustrated paying a daily or monthly rate for project management during periods when little visible construction activity is happening.

A participant working as an interior designer noted that construction budgets often run over and then cut into the furnishings budget, leaving clients unable to complete their vision, and was trying to get builders to adopt pricing certainty thinking.
A participant asked how to handle situations where the architect is behind schedule when using a fixed monthly fee model, since the client bears the cost of the delay.
A participant asked whether copies of subcontractor and vendor invoices and receipts should be included with every draw submission to the client.
A participant asked whether builders should track the global project budget including design and furnishings fees or only the construction portion.
notable_quotes: On managing modifications: 'We called her the mod queen. She was super pumped about that label. Not really.'

On pricing certainty: 'I hate being wrong. I don't know if it's my age now or what, but I used to try to give ranges. There are so many variables that I just don't give a range anymore, and people actually feel more comfortable with the budget.'
On client overconfidence in budgets: 'Clients think that just because it's on a plan it's like done. But it's not. We have to open the book up for them.'

On being candid with clients: 'The general contractor takes on the most risk out of anybody in the continuum of what a new home build is. The only way you can get trust is being really candid from the beginning. If someone wants to go with a builder who tells them yeah I could build for 200,000 less than what it really takes, fine, go do that, enjoy that journey, because that's not me.'

On building architect relationships: 'I stalked them on Instagram. I would ask a landscape architect to introduce me to an architect. I would work my trade base heavily. I was very much studying the architects and designers work and just trying to learn from these amazing people. And I still do that.'

On financial controls in spreadsheets: 'If there was an error in a formula I had little checks all throughout where things would balance. If an error went wrong it would have shown up in one of the check places and wouldn't have zeroed out in one of the ghost columns I had where everything would show zero.'
